goal: Land `P1-W3-F08` (progressive startup): the `ucil-daemon` MCP server accepts queries within 2 seconds of daemon start AND a priority indexing queue serves recently-queried files first. Ship a new `priority_queue` module (recency-ordered queue keyed by `(Instant, PathBuf)`), a new `startup` module (orchestrator that spawns the MCP server + primes the priority queue with queried-file hints and exposes a `ready` future), and the frozen module-root acceptance test `server::test_progressive_startup` that measures the startup-to-first-response latency and the priority-ordering invariant end-to-end.
plan_summary: Progressive startup, per master-plan §18 Phase 1 Week 3 line 1745 and §21.2 lines 2196-2204, has two observable invariants: (1) the MCP stdio server accepts and responds to `tools/list` within 2 s of the daemon-start call; (2) files that were 'recently queried' (by any inbound MCP `tools/call` whose params point at a file) are indexed before files that were never queried. We implement both with two small, cycle-free modules plus one acceptance test in `server.rs`. `crates/ucil-daemon/src/priority_queue.rs` introduces `PriorityIndexingQueue` (a `Mutex<BinaryHeap<QueueEntry>>` wrapper where `QueueEntry { path: PathBuf, last_queried: std::time::Instant }` sorts newest-Instant-first) with `enqueue(path)`, `touch(path)` (= 'mark recently queried, re-enqueue at the top'), `pop()` and `peek()` helpers, and a `len()` / `is_empty()` for tests. `crates/ucil-daemon/src/startup.rs` introduces `ProgressiveStartup` with `ProgressiveStartup::new(server: McpServer, queue: Arc<PriorityIndexingQueue>) -> Self`, a `start<R,W>(self, read, write) -> (JoinHandle, ReadyHandle)` method that spawns `McpServer::serve` on a tokio task AND returns a `ReadyHandle` (an `async fn ready() -> Result<Duration, StartupError>` that resolves after the server's first successful `tools/list` response with the measured wall-clock from `start()` kick-off), plus a `STARTUP_DEADLINE: Duration = Duration::from_millis(2000)` named constant tied to master-plan §21.2 phase 1 (0-2s). The startup orchestrator does NOT spawn the background indexer in this WO — that's a later integration concern (§10.2 step 7) — but it DOES call `queue.touch(path)` whenever a `tools/call` request's arguments contain a `current_task.files` entry (CEQP §8.2 universal), exercising the priority-ordering invariant without requiring a real indexer. Module-root flat tests per DEC-0005; the frozen selector `server::test_progressive_startup` resolves because the test lives at module root in `server.rs` as `#[cfg(test)] #[tokio::test] async fn test_progressive_startup()`. Real `tokio::io::duplex` pair drives the MCP exchange end-to-end (no mocks of the server). Wall-clock measured with `std::time::Instant::now()` both on the `ProgressiveStartup::start` return and on the inbound first-response frame; asserted strictly < `STARTUP_DEADLINE`.
scope_in: crates/ucil-daemon/src/priority_queue.rs — new module. `#![allow(clippy::module_name_repetitions)]` ONLY if pedantic trips on `PriorityIndexingQueue`/`QueueEntry`; prefer a rename otherwise. Rustdoc citations: master-plan §18 Phase 1 Week 3 line 1745 + §21.2 lines 2196-2204.

crates/ucil-daemon/src/priority_queue.rs — `#[derive(Debug, Clone, PartialEq, Eq)] pub struct QueueEntry { pub path: std::path::PathBuf, pub last_queried: std::time::Instant }` with `Ord`/`PartialOrd` implemented so that newest-`last_queried`-first is the pop order of a `std::collections::BinaryHeap` (i.e., `Ord` returns `other.last_queried.cmp(&self.last_queried)` — BinaryHeap is max-heap, so reversing the comparison yields the max-`Instant`-at-top invariant). The unit-test `test_queue_entry_ord_is_newest_first` pins the `Ord` direction.

crates/ucil-daemon/src/priority_queue.rs — `#[derive(Debug, Default)] pub struct PriorityIndexingQueue { inner: std::sync::Mutex<std::collections::BinaryHeap<QueueEntry>> }` with methods `new()`, `enqueue(&self, path: PathBuf)` (inserts with `Instant::now()`), `touch(&self, path: PathBuf)` (alias for enqueue — 're-prioritise' by pushing a fresh `Instant`; the stale older entry stays in the heap but will be popped later, consistent with 'recently queried first'), `pop(&self) -> Option<QueueEntry>`, `peek(&self) -> Option<QueueEntry>` (clones the top entry), `len(&self) -> usize`, `is_empty(&self) -> bool`. All methods acquire the mutex; `#[tracing::instrument(level = "trace", skip(self), fields(path = %path.display()))]` on `enqueue`/`touch` (span names `ucil.daemon.priority_queue.enqueue` / `ucil.daemon.priority_queue.touch` per §15.2). Mutex poisoning is propagated by mapping to a `PriorityQueueError::Poisoned` thiserror variant surfaced ONLY on the `.lock()` error path (the public API returns `Option`/`usize`, so poisoning is handled internally via `.unwrap_or_else(|e| e.into_inner())` on a `PoisonError` — acceptable because the heap state at the poisoning point is still consistent for reads; cite a comment referencing the `std::sync::PoisonError::into_inner` docs).
crates/ucil-daemon/src/priority_queue.rs — Module-root flat tests (NO `mod tests { }` wrapper) per DEC-0005. Exact test names (frozen selectors):

* `test_queue_entry_ord_is_newest_first` — build two `QueueEntry`s with `Instant::now()` + `Instant::now() - Duration::from_secs(1)`, push both into a `BinaryHeap`, assert `peek()` returns the newer entry.
* `test_enqueue_and_pop_single_path` — enqueue one path, assert `len() == 1`, `pop()` returns the same path, `len() == 0`.

* `test_touch_reorders_by_recency` — enqueue path A at t0, sleep 2 ms (enough to produce a strictly-later `Instant` on all platforms), enqueue path B at t1, assert `pop()` returns B then A; `touch(A)` after both enqueues should cause the NEXT `pop()` to return A (the re-touched path) before the older B entry (see the H1 note: the stale B entry remains in the heap — pop twice: first pop = A (newest touch), second pop = B (original enqueue), and the stale A entry surfaces last).
* `test_len_tracks_entries` — enqueue 3 paths, assert `len() == 3` and `is_empty() == false`; pop all three, assert `len() == 0` and `is_empty() == true`.
* `test_peek_does_not_consume` — enqueue one path, call `peek()` twice, assert both return `Some(_)` with same path and `len()` stays at 1.

crates/ucil-daemon/src/startup.rs — new module. Rustdoc citations: master-plan §10.2 lines 998-1008 (startup sequence, step 8 = MCP server goes live), §21.2 lines 2196-2204 (Phase 1 budget 0-2s), and §18 Phase 1 Week 3 line 1745 (the authoritative feature spec).

crates/ucil-daemon/src/startup.rs — `pub const STARTUP_DEADLINE: std::time::Duration = std::time::Duration::from_millis(2000);` named constant — the 2s budget from master-plan §21.2 Phase 1 (and the F08 description wording 'within 2 seconds').

crates/ucil-daemon/src/startup.rs — `#[derive(Debug, thiserror::Error)] #[non_exhaustive] pub enum StartupError` with variants `#[error("startup deadline exceeded after {elapsed_ms} ms (budget = {budget_ms} ms)")] DeadlineExceeded { elapsed_ms: u64, budget_ms: u64 }`, `#[error("server task panicked")] ServerTaskJoin(#[from] tokio::task::JoinError)`, `#[error("mcp transport error: {0}")] Mcp(#[from] crate::server::McpError)`.

crates/ucil-daemon/src/startup.rs — `pub struct ProgressiveStartup { server: crate::server::McpServer, queue: std::sync::Arc<crate::priority_queue::PriorityIndexingQueue> }` + `impl ProgressiveStartup { pub fn new(server: crate::server::McpServer, queue: std::sync::Arc<crate::priority_queue::PriorityIndexingQueue>) -> Self }`.

crates/ucil-daemon/src/startup.rs — `impl ProgressiveStartup { pub fn start<R,W>(self, reader: R, writer: W) -> (tokio::task::JoinHandle<Result<(), crate::server::McpError>>, ReadyHandle) where R: tokio::io::AsyncRead + Unpin + Send + 'static, W: tokio::io::AsyncWrite + Unpin + Send + 'static }`. Captures `start_instant = Instant::now()` before the `tokio::spawn`. The `ReadyHandle` owns a `tokio::sync::oneshot::Receiver<std::time::Duration>` that the internals resolve on the server's first successful `tools/list` response; in THIS WO the oneshot is signalled from a thin adapter that intercepts server writes — see the `with_ready_probe` helper below. `#[tracing::instrument(level = "info", skip(self, reader, writer), fields(queue_len = self.queue.len()))]`; span name `ucil.daemon.startup.start`.

crates/ucil-daemon/src/startup.rs — `pub struct ReadyHandle { rx: tokio::sync::oneshot::Receiver<std::time::Duration> }` + `impl ReadyHandle { pub async fn wait(self) -> Result<std::time::Duration, StartupError> }` that wraps the oneshot receive in `tokio::time::timeout(STARTUP_DEADLINE + Duration::from_millis(500), ...)` (we use a slightly-over-budget timeout so the `DeadlineExceeded` error path is reachable inside `wait` rather than in a caller; the surrounding assertion in the acceptance test uses the RETURNED duration, not the timeout).

crates/ucil-daemon/src/startup.rs — `fn with_ready_probe<W>(writer: W, start: std::time::Instant, tx: tokio::sync::oneshot::Sender<std::time::Duration>) -> ReadyProbeWriter<W> where W: tokio::io::AsyncWrite + Unpin` — a private adapter that wraps the outbound `AsyncWrite`; on the first successful `poll_write` that carries a complete JSON-RPC response frame (detected by the last byte being `\n` AND the inbound body containing `"id"`), it sends `Instant::now() - start` through `tx` (once; subsequent writes pass through unchanged). `ReadyProbeWriter` implements `AsyncWrite` via delegation. This adapter exists ONLY in this module; consumers reach it via `ProgressiveStartup::start`.

crates/ucil-daemon/src/startup.rs — `pub fn handle_call_for_priority<'a>(queue: &crate::priority_queue::PriorityIndexingQueue, arguments: &'a serde_json::Value)` — free helper that walks `arguments.current_task.files_in_context` (the CEQP universal per master-plan §8.2 and the existing `ceqp_input_schema` in `server.rs`) and calls `queue.touch(path)` for each string entry. Returns `()`. Unit-tested at module root.
crates/ucil-daemon/src/startup.rs — Module-root flat tests (NO `mod tests { }` wrapper). Exact names:
* `test_startup_deadline_constant_is_2s` — `assert_eq!(STARTUP_DEADLINE, Duration::from_millis(2000));`.

* `test_handle_call_for_priority_touches_files_in_context` — build a `PriorityIndexingQueue`, construct a `serde_json::json!({ "current_task": { "files_in_context": ["/tmp/a.rs", "/tmp/b.rs"] } })` and assert after the helper runs, `queue.len() == 2` and `pop()` returns paths `/tmp/b.rs` then `/tmp/a.rs` (the LAST array entry was touched last so it is newest — pin the ordering contract).
* `test_handle_call_for_priority_ignores_missing_files_key` — pass a `json!({ "reason": "x" })` object (no `current_task.files_in_context`) and assert `queue.is_empty()` afterwards.

* `test_ready_handle_resolves_on_first_write` — build a `ReadyProbeWriter` wrapping a `tokio::io::duplex` write-half; write one framed JSON-RPC response ending in `\n`; assert the paired `ReadyHandle::wait()` future resolves with a `Duration` that is <= 500 ms. Uses `tokio::time::timeout` as an outer cap.

crates/ucil-daemon/src/lib.rs — add `pub mod priority_queue;` and `pub mod startup;` module declarations (keep module order alphabetical where possible; the existing block is lifecycle/plugin_manager/server/session_manager/session_ttl/storage/watcher — so insert `priority_queue` between `plugin_manager` and `server`, and `startup` between `session_ttl` and `storage`). Extend the module-level rustdoc paragraph with ONE sentence per new module citing master-plan §18 Phase 1 Week 3 line 1745 for `startup` and §21.2 lines 2196-2204 for `priority_queue`. Add matching `pub use` re-export blocks after the existing ones (alphabetical): `pub use priority_queue::{PriorityIndexingQueue, QueueEntry};` and `pub use startup::{ProgressiveStartup, ReadyHandle, StartupError, STARTUP_DEADLINE};`. NO other edits to lib.rs (the `#![deny(warnings)]` + `#![warn(clippy::all, clippy::pedantic, clippy::nursery)]` + `#![deny(rustdoc::broken_intra_doc_links)]` regime stays byte-for-byte).

crates/ucil-daemon/src/server.rs — add ONE new module-root test `#[cfg(test)] #[tokio::test] async fn test_progressive_startup()` at the bottom of the file (AFTER the existing `test_ceqp_params_on_all_tools` but before any existing tail comments; keep the file ordering so the two prior module-root tests stay contiguous per the WO-0011/0016/0023 precedent). The test:
* Creates a `PriorityIndexingQueue` and an `McpServer::new()`, wires them through `ProgressiveStartup::new`.
* Spins up a `tokio::io::duplex(16 * 1024)` pair; calls `startup.start(server_read, server_write)` and captures BOTH the server `JoinHandle` and the `ReadyHandle`.
* In parallel (on the client side) writes one `tools/list` request frame.
* Awaits `ready_handle.wait()` with an outer `tokio::time::timeout(Duration::from_secs(3), ...)` cap, asserts the returned `Duration` is < `STARTUP_DEADLINE` (the master-plan §21.2 Phase 1 budget).

* Reads the inbound response frame, parses it, asserts the `tools` array has exactly `TOOL_COUNT == 22` entries — same contract the pre-existing `test_all_22_tools_registered` asserts, re-used here to prove the server is fully wired during the startup window.

* Calls `handle_call_for_priority(&queue, &json!({ "current_task": { "files_in_context": ["src/a.rs", "src/b.rs"] } }))` and asserts `queue.pop().unwrap().path == PathBuf::from("src/b.rs")` AND the next `queue.pop().unwrap().path == PathBuf::from("src/a.rs")` — the priority-ordering invariant from the feature description 'serves recently-queried files first'.
* Cleanly shuts down the client write half, joins the server task with `tokio::time::timeout(Duration::from_secs(3), ...)`.

crates/ucil-daemon/src/server.rs — ONLY additions. NO edits to any existing `McpServer` method, no edits to `ucil_tools()`, no edits to `ceqp_input_schema()`, no edits to the two pre-existing module-root tests.

Every `.await` on IO wrapped in `tokio::time::timeout` (rust-style rule + phase-log invariant 7) — the `ReadyHandle::wait` helper wraps its oneshot receive in `timeout(STARTUP_DEADLINE + 500ms, ...)`; the outer acceptance test wraps server-task-join and ready-handle-await in 3s/ready-handle-local timeouts.

`StartupError` is `#[derive(thiserror::Error)]` with `#[non_exhaustive]`; `ProgressiveStartup::start` returns a pair `(JoinHandle, ReadyHandle)` rather than coupling them (the error surface is narrow; the caller opts-in to waiting for ready).

Rustdoc on every new public item cites master-plan §18 Phase 1 Week 3 line 1745 + §21.2 lines 2196-2204 + §10.2 lines 998-1008 as appropriate. `cargo doc -p ucil-daemon --no-deps` must exit clean — `lib.rs:34` has `#![deny(rustdoc::broken_intra_doc_links)]`, so rustdoc errors are build errors. WO-0025's lesson (`[`symbol_count()`]` with trailing `()` to disambiguate salsa-tracked fn/struct) does NOT apply here — no salsa-tracked items in `priority_queue` / `startup`. But: any intra-doc link to `McpServer` / `McpError` MUST be plain `[`McpServer`]` (function form only) — no `struct@` prefix needed.

Clippy pedantic + nursery are in effect (`lib.rs:33`). Expect `must_use_candidate`, `missing_errors_doc`, `missing_const_for_fn`, `module_name_repetitions` on the additions. Fix, don't blanket-allow. `#[allow(clippy::module_name_repetitions)]` is permissible at module top ONLY for `priority_queue.rs` / `startup.rs` IF pedantic trips on `PriorityIndexingQueue` / `ProgressiveStartup` / `ReadyHandle` / `QueueEntry`; document the `#[allow]` with a comment citing the `watcher.rs:39` precedent.

`PriorityIndexingQueue` uses `std::sync::Mutex`, NOT `tokio::sync::Mutex`. Rationale: the queue's methods are synchronous (no `.await` under the lock), and `std::sync::Mutex` gives us easy `Send` across the `Arc<>` boundary. The tokio-docs guidance (`tokio::sync::Mutex` when holding across .await) does NOT apply to our call-site. Handle `PoisonError` by calling `.into_inner()` — the heap invariant is consistent at poisoning points because every mutation is a single heap insert/pop.

The priority-queue ordering test (`test_touch_reorders_by_recency`) must sleep > 1 ms between `enqueue` calls because `std::time::Instant` resolution on Linux is typically 1 ns but on macOS it can be coarser — a 2 ms gap is enough to produce a strictly-later Instant on all tier-1 CI targets and still keeps the test under 10 ms.

`handle_call_for_priority` touches paths in the ORDER they appear in the `files_in_context` array. The ordering contract tested in `test_handle_call_for_priority_touches_files_in_context` is 'last touched = newest = popped first'. Do NOT change the iteration order without updating the test.

Conventional Commits with `Phase: 1`, `Feature: P1-W3-F08`, `Work-order: WO-0028` trailers on every commit; push after every commit; no `--amend` after push; no force-push. Target 5 commits, ~450 lines total (priority_queue types + tests; startup types + helpers; startup probe-writer + ready-handle; server.rs test; lib.rs re-exports).

Anti-abandonment safeguard (learned from WO-0012/0020/0024) — before ending ANY session on this WO, the executor MUST verify: (a) `git log origin/main..HEAD --oneline` shows ≥ 4 commits on the feature branch, (b) `git diff origin/main..HEAD -- crates/ucil-daemon/src/priority_queue.rs | wc -l` returns > 80, (c) `git diff origin/main..HEAD -- crates/ucil-daemon/src/startup.rs | wc -l` returns > 140, (d) `git diff origin/main..HEAD -- crates/ucil-daemon/src/server.rs | wc -l` returns > 20, (e) `git status --porcelain` is clean, (f) `git rev-parse HEAD` == `git rev-parse @{upstream}` (branch pushed), AND (g) `ucil-build/work-orders/0028-ready-for-review.md` exists. If any check fails, either keep working or write an escalation.

Reality-check multi-commit false-positive — `scripts/reality-check.sh P1-W3-F08` may trip the documented multi-commit rollback heuristic (escalation `20260415-1630-reality-check-per-file-rollback.md`). If so, the verifier performs the manual two-step mutation instead (rolling back `startup.rs` + `priority_queue.rs` + the new `server.rs` test + lib.rs declarations, asserting `server::test_progressive_startup` selector fails or returns 0 tests; restoring; asserting ≥ 1 test passes). Document this fallback expectation in `0028-ready-for-review.md`.

Coverage-gate guard (learned from WO-0026 retry 1+2) — `scripts/gate/ucil-daemon.sh` (or the equivalent per-crate coverage floor invoked by the verifier) should see a net-positive line-count of real code added to `crates/ucil-daemon/src/**`. Both new modules carry full unit-test coverage at module root, so the ratio should improve, not regress. If the gate regresses, treat it as a signal that testing is too thin on one of the two new modules — NOT a signal to lower the floor.
